verses.text: Samson went down to Timnath, and saw a woman in Timnath of the daughters of the Philistines. He came up, and told his father and his mother, and said, I have seen a woman in Timnath of the daughters of the Philistines: now therefore get her for me to wife. His father and his mother said unto him, Is there never a woman among the daughters of thy brethren, or among all my people, that thou goest to take a wife of the uncircumcised Philistines? And Samson said unto his father, Get her for me; for she pleaseth me well. Guidance: 'I have seen… get her for me… she pleaseth me well.' Three verbs of the eyes and the heart, none of the head or the covenant. Samson's introductory speech contains the seed of his whole life's pattern. His parents push back gently with the right question (Deut 7:3 forbade exactly this marriage), and he overrides them. The first words of Samson's adult career are 'get her' — a demanding child in a grown man's body. The Spirit will move on him in chapter 14; the heart that asked first will not always wait for the Spirit's verdict.

And Samson said unto his father, Get her for me; for she pleaseth me well. Guidance: 'I have seen… get her for me… she pleaseth me well.' Three verbs of the eyes and the heart, none of the head or the covenant. Samson's introductory speech contains the seed of his whole life's pattern. But his father and his mother knew not that it was of the LORD, that he sought an occasion against the Philistines: for at that time the Philistines had dominion over Israel. Guidance: The narrator drops the chapter's most important line as a parenthesis. The LORD was using Samson's disobedience as His weapon against Philistia. Samson's mixed motives did not derail the divine plan; God can drive His own purposes even through a man chasing the wrong woman. This is not a license to repeat Samson's choices — it is a window into how God works. Sovereignty over sin is not the same as sponsorship of it. He uses what He never commanded.

Then went Samson down, and his father and his mother, to Timnath, and came to the vineyards of Timnath: and, behold, a young lion roared against him. The Spirit of the LORD came mightily upon him, and he rent him as he would have rent a kid, and he had nothing in his hand: but he told not his father or his mother what he had done. Guidance: First sign of trouble inside the calling: 'the vineyards of Timnath.' A Nazirite was not to touch grapes (Num 6:3); the location alone is a vow-violation. Then a lion. The Spirit comes 'mightily' (the verb is intense) and Samson tears it open with empty hands. But he hides what just happened from his parents. The Spirit-empowered miracle is filed under secret. Why? Perhaps because explaining the lion in the vineyard would require explaining why he was in the vineyard. Secrecy starts where vows have begun to crack.

Then went Samson down, and his father and his mother, to Timnath, and came to the vineyards of Timnath: and, behold, a young lion roared against him. The Spirit of the LORD came mightily upon him, and he rent him as he would have rent a kid, and he had nothing in his hand: but he told not his father or his mother what he had done. Guidance: First sign of trouble inside the calling: 'the vineyards of Timnath.' A Nazirite was not to touch grapes (Num 6:3); the location alone is a vow-violation. Then a lion. The Spirit comes 'mightily' (the verb is intense) and Samson tears it open with empty hands. But he hides what just happened from his parents. The Spirit-empowered miracle is filed under secret. Why? Perhaps because explaining the lion in the vineyard would require explaining why he was in the vineyard. Secrecy starts where vows have begun to crack.

He went down, and talked with the woman; and she pleased Samson well. After a time he returned to take her, and he turned aside to see the carcase of the lion: and, behold, there was a swarm of bees and honey in the carcase of the lion. He took thereof in his hands, and went on eating, and came to his father and mother, and he gave them, and they did eat: but he told not them that he had taken the honey out of the carcase of the lion. Guidance: A Nazirite was also not to touch a dead body. Samson scoops honey out of a corpse and feeds it to his unsuspecting parents. He defiles them too — and again 'he told not.' Each new sin trains the next: first the vineyard, then the corpse, then dragging the parents in by deceit. The honey is sweet; the means is unclean. The riddle he will craft tonight is born here — but so is the moral drift the rest of his life will dramatize.

So his father went down unto the woman: and Samson made there a feast; for so used the young men to do. It came to pass, when they saw him, that they brought thirty companions to be with him. Guidance: A seven-day Philistine wedding feast — the standard format. 'Companions' is a polite word for the bride's family's chaperones, who served partly as security in case the foreign groom turned out badly. The thirty are outnumbered by Samson and his strength; the bride's family is hedging. The chapter's body count begins arithmetically with this number.

So his father went down unto the woman: and Samson made there a feast; for so used the young men to do. It came to pass, when they saw him, that they brought thirty companions to be with him. Guidance: A seven-day Philistine wedding feast — the standard format. 'Companions' is a polite word for the bride's family's chaperones, who served partly as security in case the foreign groom turned out badly. The thirty are outnumbered by Samson and his strength; the bride's family is hedging. The chapter's body count begins arithmetically with this number.

Samson said unto them, I will now put forth a riddle unto you: if ye can certainly declare it me within the seven days of the feast, and find it out, then I will give you thirty sheets and thirty change of garments: but if ye cannot declare it me, then shall ye give me thirty sheets and thirty change of garments. They said unto him, Put forth thy riddle, that we may hear it. He said unto them, Out of the eater came forth meat, and out of the strong came forth sweetness. They could not in three days expound the riddle. Guidance: Samson the showman. The riddle is impossible without inside information — and that is the trap. Honey and a lion are not paired anywhere outside this one corpse. He has set a bet they can only win by spying. Thirty linen wraps and thirty changes of clothes is a fortune. Samson is gambling with vow-defilement spoils. Even his riddles are confessions of the secret sins behind them — 'out of the eater came forth meat' is, in plain prose, 'I took honey out of a corpse against my vow.' He is hiding in plain sight.

It came to pass on the seventh day, that they said unto Samson's wife, Entice thy husband, that he may declare unto us the riddle, lest we burn thee and thy father's house with fire: have ye called us to take that we have? is it not so? Samson's wife wept before him, and said, Thou dost but hate me, and lovest me not: thou hast put forth a riddle unto the children of my people, and hast not told it me. He said unto her, Behold, I have not told it my father nor my mother, and shall I tell it thee? She wept before him the seven days, while their feast lasted: it came to pass on the seventh day, that he told her, because she lay sore upon him: and she told the riddle to the children of her people. Guidance: The chaperones turn on the bride — threaten to burn her family alive. Coercion runs through the same logic as Pharaoh, Saul, and Delilah later: terrify a woman, steal a secret. The wife weeps seven days; on the seventh day Samson cracks. The pattern is being established: a strong man with no defense against tears. Samson's giftings are physical; his weakness is relational. He will lose his eyes in chapter 16 to a different woman doing essentially the same thing with the same playbook. He never learns.

The men of the city said unto him on the seventh day before the sun went down, What is sweeter than honey? what is stronger than a lion? And he said unto them, If ye had not plowed with my heifer, ye had not found out my riddle. The Spirit of the LORD came upon him, and he went down to Ashkelon, and slew thirty men of them, and took their spoil, and gave change of garments unto them which expounded the riddle. His anger was kindled, and he went up to his father's house. Guidance: Their answer is a rhyming Hebrew couplet — coy on the surface, smug underneath. Samson's retort is cruder: 'if ye had not plowed with my heifer' — degrading both his wife and the cheaters in one image. Then the Spirit comes upon him again, but for a darker purpose: a raid on Ashkelon, twenty miles away, killing thirty strangers to pay the bet from their dead bodies' clothes. Samson's gift and his temper are now linked. The Spirit empowers a man whose motives are wounded pride. God uses it (the chapter has told us — 'occasion against the Philistines') but the human cost is real and unjust. The narrative does not glamorize this verse; it documents it.

But Samson's wife was given to his companion, whom he had used as his friend. Guidance: One sentence and a marriage is over. Samson stormed off; the bride's father assumed the marriage was annulled and gave her to the best man. The bride disappears from Samson's life into a household that betrayed her under threat. The chapter ends on a personal disaster that will trigger the next chapter's destruction. Choices made by men of moods produce wreckage that women bear most. The 'occasion against the Philistines' the LORD was generating has cost a daughter her future as collateral.
